lessons.title: Explicit Object Parameter: Syntax and Basic Patterns
lessons.motivation: In C++17, writing a getter that works correctly across all combinations of const-ness and value category required duplicating the method body into two or even four overloads. A simple field accessor needs T& get() &, const T& get() const&, T&& get() && and const T&& get() const&&. All four carry identical logic — only the qualifiers differ. Any change to the logic must be applied in multiple places, leading to bugs and bloated code. C++23 solves this with the explicit object parameter (also called "deducing this"): the object qualifier becomes an ordinary template parameter, and the compiler deduces its type automatically at each call site.
lessons.theory:
An explicit object parameter is declared as the first parameter of a non-static method with the keyword this before the name: auto& get(this auto& self). The parameter is conventionally named self, though any name is valid. The type of self is deduced by the compiler using template argument deduction rules based on the value category of the object the method is called on: if the object is an lvalue of type Widget, Self = Widget; if it is a const lvalue, Self = const Widget; if it is an rvalue, Self = Widget (no ampersand, but the reference binding on self is an rvalue reference). The compiler instantiates a concrete type at every call site, exactly as with a function template.

The call syntax is unchanged: w.get() works exactly as before. The compiler handles passing the object into self. An explicit object parameter cannot be combined with virtual or static — the method must be an ordinary non-static member.

The main practical benefit is eliminating const/non-const overload duplication. Instead of four variants of get(), one suffices: auto&& get(this auto&& self). Here self is deduced to the appropriate reference category automatically. To return a member while preserving the value category of the object, use std::forward_like<decltype(self)>(self.member): this C++23 utility applies the same const-ness and reference kind to the member as self carries.

For method chaining (fluent interfaces, builder pattern) the explicit object parameter is equally useful: auto& set_x(this auto& self, X val) returns self, and the next method can be called immediately on that reference. The return type adapts automatically to the actual type of the object, which matters in inheritance — a derived class gets a reference to itself, not to the base.

Value categories of self: when the parameter is declared as this auto& self, self is always an lvalue reference, and passing an rvalue object is a compile error. To handle rvalues, declare this auto&& self — a forwarding reference that binds to any value category. Inside the method, use std::forward<Self>(self) to perfectly forward self itself, where Self is the deduced type.

Restrictions: a method with an explicit object parameter cannot be declared virtual — virtual dispatch is incompatible with template type deduction. You also cannot add &, &&, or const qualifiers to the method itself — all of that is now expressed through the type of self.

examples.title: Basic syntax: one method instead of two overloads
examples.explanation: The method get accepts self as a reference to auto. For b (Box&), Self is deduced as Box, and the method returns std::string&, allowing assignment. For cb (const Box&), Self is deduced as const Box, and the method returns const std::string& — writing through it would be a compile error. One method covers both cases without duplicating the body.

examples.title: Eliminating four overloads: std::forward_like
examples.explanation: The this auto&& self parameter is a forwarding reference, deduced as int& for b, const int& for cb, and int&& for the temporary Buffer{}. std::forward_like<decltype(self)>(self.data) propagates to the member data the same const-ness and value category that self carries: for an rvalue object, data is returned as int&&, enabling a move rather than a copy. In C++17 this required four separate overloads.

examples.title: Fluent builder: method chaining via explicit object parameter
examples.explanation: The methods set_host and set_port accept self by lvalue reference and return it. The return type auto& is deduced as Config&, exactly what is needed to continue the chain. In an inheritance scenario the same technique automatically returns a reference to the derived class rather than the base, something that in C++17 required CRTP or separate implementations in every subclass.

lessons.title: Recursive Lambdas and CRTP Replacement

background.title: CRTP in C++17
background.body: CRTP (Curiously Recurring Template Pattern) is a C++17 idiom where a base class is parameterized with the derived class: template<typename Derived> struct Base { ... }; struct Concrete : Base<Concrete> { ... };. This lets Base call methods on Concrete via static_cast<Derived*>(this) without virtual function overhead — enabling static polymorphism and the mixin pattern. The drawbacks are a verbose template parameter on every base class, an unsafe downcast that causes UB if the hierarchy is wrong, and non-obvious syntax that makes the code harder to read.

lessons.motivation: Before C++23, a recursive lambda required either wrapping it in std::function — incurring heap allocation and type erasure overhead — or using the Y-combinator trick, which is clever but hard to read. The CRTP pattern for static polymorphism and mixins also suffered from verbose template parameters and an unsafe downcast. The explicit object parameter (deducing this) solves both problems: a lambda can refer to itself via a self parameter without std::function, and a base class can call methods on the derived type without a template parameter and without any cast.
lessons.theory:
Before C++23, recursive lambdas were written by capturing themselves through a std::function<int(int)> wrapper, which required a heap allocation and type erasure. The alternative was the Y-combinator — a higher-order function that passes the lambda to itself — but its syntax is difficult to follow. With the explicit object parameter, a lambda receives its own closure type as a deduced template parameter self and can call itself directly: auto fib = [](this auto self, int n) -> int { return n <= 1 ? n : self(n-1) + self(n-2); };. The parameter this auto self means: "deduce the closure type and pass it as self by value." There is no overhead — the compiler instantiates a concrete function just like any template. The explicit return type annotation (-> int) is required here because the compiler cannot deduce the return type of a recursive body automatically.

The CRTP replacement works differently. In classic CRTP the base class knows the derived type through a template parameter and reaches it via static_cast<Derived*>(this). This is unsafe: a wrong hierarchy makes the cast undefined behavior. With deducing this the base class needs no template parameter at all: void interface(this auto& self) { self.implementation(); }. When derived.interface() is called, the compiler deduces Self = Derived and passes the object as self — the call self.implementation() resolves on the actual type without any cast. If the derived class does not define implementation(), you get a clear compile error. If it does, the correct function is called.

The Overloaded pattern for std::visit is another application of variadic base classes. The template template<typename... Ts> struct Overloaded : Ts... { using Ts::operator()...; }; aggregates multiple lambdas and exposes all their operator() in a single type. std::visit dispatches the value stored in a variant to the matching operator() by type. This is cleaner than a chain of if-else on std::holds_alternative and safer than switching on the index: the compiler verifies that all variant alternatives are handled.

All three idioms share a single mechanism: the compiler deduces the exact type at the call site and instantiates a concrete function, freeing the developer from explicit casts, template parameters, and helper wrappers. The resulting code is shorter, reads like an ordinary method call, and is fully checked by the type system.

examples.title: Recursive lambda: Fibonacci without std::function
examples.explanation: The parameter this auto self means the closure type of the lambda itself is deduced and passed in self by value. Inside the body, self(n-1) calls the same lambda recursively. The explicit return type -> int is required: without it the compiler cannot deduce the return type from a recursive body. Compared to std::function there is no heap allocation and no type erasure — the compiler generates an ordinary template function.

examples.title: CRTP replacement: Printable mixin without a template parameter
examples.explanation: Printable knows nothing about Cat or Dog — it has no template parameter. When c.print_info() is called, the compiler deduces Self = Cat and passes c in self. The call self.describe() resolves to Cat::describe() — no downcast, no UB. When d.print_info() is called, Self is deduced as Dog. If any subclass had omitted describe(), the compiler would report an error at the print_info() call site, not deep inside template machinery. This is cleaner than classic CRTP: no template<typename Derived> on the base class, no static_cast.

examples.title: Overloaded for std::visit: variant visitor without if-else
examples.explanation: Overloaded inherits from all the supplied types (here three lambdas) and exposes all their operator() via using. std::visit looks at the active type in the variant and calls the matching operator() on the visitor. The compiler verifies that every possible variant type is handled — a missing case is a compile error. This is safer than switching on std::variant::index() and reads like a table: each case is one line. The pattern works without deducing this, but it illustrates the same principle: aggregating behavior without virtual functions and without any explicit downcast.

lessons.title: Advanced Patterns: Chain Builders, std::variant, Limitations

background.title: std::variant and std::visit (C++17)
background.body: std::variant<T...> holds exactly one of its listed types at a time. std::visit(visitor, v) calls the visitor with the currently stored type — which traditionally required either overloaded operator() s or a templated visitor. C++20 concepts: the requires syntax lets you constrain a template parameter — the Self in an explicit object parameter can be constrained the same way.

lessons.motivation: After mastering the basic syntax and recursive lambdas, natural follow-up questions arise: how should you design a builder pattern — return by reference or by value? Can you use an explicit object parameter inside a stateful visitor for std::variant? And where exactly are the limits of deducing this — what is forbidden and why? Understanding these details is essential for using the feature confidently in production code.
lessons.theory:
The central design question for builder patterns with deducing this is: should the method take this auto& self (lvalue reference) or this auto self (by value)? The difference is significant. A method with this auto& self accepts the object by lvalue reference and returns it — this is efficient, but means that chaining on a temporary fails: Widget{}.set_a(1).set_b(2) produces a dangling reference because set_a returns a reference to an already-destroyed temporary. A method with this auto self accepts the object by value (copies it) and returns a new copy — chaining on rvalues works correctly. The choice depends on context: for mutable lvalue objects the reference variant is preferred; for immutable builders or APIs where copying is acceptable, the by-value variant is the right pick. In either case, inheritance works automatically: the derived class gets Self = Derived without CRTP.

A stateful visitor for std::variant using deducing this is another practical pattern. Instead of an Overloaded structure assembling several lambdas, you can write a single struct with multiple operator() overloads, each taking this auto& self. The compiler selects the most specialized operator() based on the type held in the variant: if there is an operator() for int and a generic one for auto, a call with an int goes to the specialized version. The visitor's state lives directly in the struct's fields and is updated through self — no external closure, no captured references.

Knowing the limitations of the explicit object parameter saves time that would otherwise go to searching for non-existent solutions. First, such a method cannot be virtual: virtual dispatch requires a fixed function address in the vtable, while the template parameter Self produces a distinct instantiation per type — the two mechanisms are incompatible. Second, the method cannot be static: a static method exists without an object, but an explicit object parameter is precisely about describing the object. Third, you cannot simultaneously add &, &&, or const qualifiers to the method itself — they are now expressed through the type of self, and duplicating them is meaningless: void foo(this auto& self) const is a compile error. Fourth, in C++23 the explicit object parameter cannot be applied to operator=, operator->, constructors, or destructors — a constructor simply has no existing object to deduce a type from.

C++20 concepts combine naturally with deducing this: the type of self can be constrained by a concept directly in the signature — this HasDescribe auto const& self. This is more compact than a separate requires-clause and expresses intent more precisely: "the method print is only available to types that have a describe() method." The compile error appears at the call site g.print() for an unsuitable type, with a clear concept-violation message rather than a cryptic template-instantiation failure buried deep in the error log. This approach enables expressive mixin libraries without virtual functions and without CRTP.

examples.title: Chain builder with value return: chaining on a temporary
examples.explanation: Each method accepts self by value (this auto self) and returns a copy with the updated field. The chain starts on a temporary Email{}: to() produces a copy with to_ filled, subject() another copy with subject_ set, and so on. No reference ever becomes dangling. If the methods returned this auto& self instead, Email{}.to(...) would return a reference to the expiring temporary, causing undefined behavior. With inheritance, Self is deduced as the derived class — methods automatically return DerivedEmail, not Email.

examples.title: Stateful std::variant visitor via explicit object parameter
examples.explanation: Counter is a stateful visitor: its fields ints and strings accumulate the result of the traversal. Three operator() overloads accept self by lvalue reference and update its fields. std::visit(c, v) passes c by reference (no copy) and calls the matching overload based on the active type in the variant. The compiler selects the most specialized operator(): the first for int, the second for std::string, the third for double. After five visits, c holds the correct counts. Without deducing this, updating the state required writing ++ints (without self) or passing an explicit this pointer.

examples.title: Concept constraint on explicit object parameter: a safe mixin
examples.explanation: HasDescribe constrains the type Self: the method print is only available to subclasses of Printable that have a describe() method returning something convertible to std::string. Writing this HasDescribe auto const& self is more compact than a separate requires-clause. If BadClass{}.print() were attempted, the compiler would immediately report a HasDescribe concept violation — a clear, readable error at the exact call site rather than a cryptic message buried in template instantiation output. This combination of deducing this with concepts enables expressive mixin interfaces without virtual functions and without the ceremony of CRTP.
